author: @VeniceInventors
text:
On one hand I really like the idea of the generator to motor bicycle as it makes it possible to set a constant pedaling effort and travel speed regardless of the changes in road inclination, on the other hand I often see situations where it would be much more efficient to avoid conversion between mechanical, heat and electrical energy. While not always possible or practical, it makes sense to locally use the mechanical power of the wind or a stream to power mechanical devices, IR heat from sun to heat air, water, food, etc, and PV panels to power electronics and lights.
Losses from transporting electricity or heat over long distances seem quite linear. Interestingly enough, mechanical power doesn't seem to be subject to the same linearity. There is a limit to how long a shaft or chain can be, but within that limit it doesn't seem to make any significant difference how much distance is covered.
I suppose one could argue that there is a linear relationship between the weight of a shaft or chain and the load on the bearings, but short of carrying several tons, it probably doesn't come anywhere near the losses from electrical resistance or thermal dissipation.

author: @koaasst

author: @korvorn
text: There actually was a mechanical power transmission technique, called Stangenkunst or Flatrod System,  used in the past that could transfer power several kilometers away using a waterwheel and pushrods!    It was invented in the 16th century and by the 18th century was being used to transmit power up to four kilometers.  I'm always amazed at the incredible cleverness of people in the past.  If you google "Stangenkunst" it will take you to a wiki about it and also a fascinating article from lowtechmagazine.

author: @whatelseison8970
text:
Quite often high voltage transmission lines use aluminum conductors instead of copper despite its lower conductance (~60% of Cu) and increased risk of metal fatigue. Aluminum is so much lighter than copper though that it can actually end up carry way more (~5.5X) power per unit _weight_ than copper.. or silver for that matter (~7X!)).  Plus you're getting even larger favorable factors when you consider capacity vs material cost for Cu vs Al. 

A larger diameter wire also experiences less corona loss at very high voltages but that's just icing on the cake. haha.

author: @CitizenSmith50
text: The Lister-driven belt system took me straight back to my youth over half a century ago, and the Outback shearing sheds I worked in in Australia, where the remoteness meant no electricity so all the shears, sharpeners and other equipment were belt-driven from a kerosene powered stationary engine ! Cheers from country South Australia !

text: caution Rob , getting people to think for themselves is quite possibly hazardous to ones health !?. It's almost at a level with people that theorize about what the uber rich power brokers, that have got uber wealthy and powerfull by being 'very competitive ', are 'putting their heads together' over now ?.  Was it Edisons original power transmission system , the use of 'rust batteries' , transport iron to homes for onsite generation ,and cart it back to central plants for recycleing . Still not a bad idea ,considering the line loses in long distance power transmission ,I think in NZ we lose around 40% . Could a replacement battery system work like gas bottles ,take them to the hydro power plant to refill ,? .

author: @bartronicsecurity
text: Only 25 years ago I saw a full production line which made shoes and running from a single spinning shaft overhead powering leather sewing machines and presses and stamp cutters to molds and more.  The plant was likely more efficient and less expensive to setup and maintain running this way than if electic motors were used everywhere for each machine.  It was amazing to see in action. The main source of power was a massive electric motor but they had plans on changing it to a powerful diesel engine as electicity was getting unreliable in Zimbabwe at the time.

author: @davidblyth5495
text:
Another excellent presentation!
When I started my career as an apprentice, we spent time in a technical college's machine shop. I remember the belts and pulleys overhead and the highly skilled, elderly instructor. That was in 1968.
Am I that old now?
I'm grateful for having seen that.
Your argument over transmission efficiency is important - the "best" depends on the application.
Thank you!
